And don't worry about new markets right now. By now you should be clear on the importance of really digging in and knowing your market, your goals. Look, you're going to have enough trouble figuring out your neighborhood, your part of this city, whether rents are the best. You have your hands full studying your market. You don't need to go across the country and study that market. To successfully buy your market, and I highly recommend you stay in your market for now. And if you can't stay in your market and you can't leave your market, you can't leave your town, but you're going to go to another market and invest, don't do it. Find somebody in that market to invest with. To successfully buy your market, you need to know where your market is right now and where it's going in the future. Then you should build a predict that by the way, precisely what constitutes a smart deal, a strategic deal, a cash flow positive deal, a good deal to exit with. You simply cannot afford to go outside of where you are at this time if you haven't learned your own market. This is the perfect plan. In the beginning, know your market only. I'll follow this to the letter. I was in San Diego, I purchased in San Diego. When I made a move to Tucson, I researched Tucson and I moved all my resources to Tucson. The obvious limitations is when you cannot leave your neighborhood. So don't go to Cleveland. Don't go to Orlando. Don't go to St. Lucy. Don't go to Miami if you haven't handled Akron. Now if there's nothing to buy in Akron or everything's overpriced where you live or the lenders aren't lending where you are because maybe they're losing jobs there, then you need to either partner with someone in another market that you're interested in and do your homework there or pick up and move to that market and go buy there. That's the beautiful thing about real estate. I can buy real estate all over the country. I've been in San Diego, Houston, Los Angeles, Austin, Texas, Miami, Tampa, Orlando, the Panhandle, Charlotte, North Carolina, Savannah, Georgia and I will continue to buy in many of those marketplaces excluding of course the Wacos and Cali. Why? Because they tax too hard in Cali. They're going to steal everything from the landlord and they're extremely pro-rinner there and anti-landlord. You're going to lose in court most of the times in California and they have water problems and they have interstate problems and they have infrastructure problems and they're probably a broken state. I just hadn't admitted it yet. Look, know your general proximity. Know your market. You're going to have your hands full again. You should really know everything there is to know about it. What's the good part of town? What's the great part of that neighborhood? That's rule one, know your market. Rule number two, don't buy anything less than 16 units. Now you're going to be tempted to buy less than 16 units because it's going to fit your budget. Don't do it. You should be looking at closer to 32 ideally. Don't sweat small deals. There are small deals. Don't spend time on them. Don't invest resources on them. Don't get started with them. I'm telling you, these are the deals that will give you more trouble than they will ever provide you with profit. And if you're not going to listen to me and do a small deal, make sure you're in and out as quickly as possible. At that point, you're not investing anymore. You're flipping. The small stuff goes down first when the economy takes a dive. My investments, the big deals will keep plugging ahead no matter what the economy looks like tomorrow and that's a good feeling. As long as I have time and cash flow, I'll get to appreciation. Again, that's a big part of the reason I say hold on to investment properties longer term, but at the same time, you have to have the cash flow in the interim to get you through the down times. Don't step off the course. Focus. Regardless of where you invest, again, within your general proximity, you need to know your market in New York. Tenets will pay for things that they would never pay for in Kansas City. So just because you can do VIP garbage in New York and they'll pay it, maybe $40, or just because they'll pay hundreds of dollars for parking does not mean they'll do that in another part of the country. In different markets, you can get away with different things. In different markets, there's different wants and needs. You have to take care of your tenant. If you know your market, you'll know what you can and can't do. If you don't, you'll be charging people in Kansas for Manhattan things and they'll laugh in your face. It's not ideal. And know your market. Know you love your market, by the way. You should love your market. For instance, I love Orlando. I love Tampa. I love Houston. I love Savannah. I actually like the Panheadle. I like almost any market in Florida. I like anywhere in the South that's warm. I love Phoenix. I don't like Tucson too much. Absolutely love Denver, but it's too expensive. Why am I telling you all this? Love your market. Your money's going to be invested there. You're going to be dealing with the tenants there. You're going to be visiting the property there. This is work. Love where you work, okay? And if you don't love where you are, make sure you make a lot of money there because it's not going to be worth it. I went there, I remember going to Orlando once. I went there and I spent time in the community. It made sense for me and my business to be there. I had an office there now for 20 years. I didn't have to rely on the reports. I got in my car and I drove around Orlando. I saw the traffic. I saw people spending money. I saw people flying into the airport. I got a report that said Orlando was number one or number two, most traveled to destination in the United States. The airport is constantly used. Jobs were coming in. There was development happening. There was roads being built. I scoped out the job market. I know what it was like to hire people there. I visited business owners and car dealerships and I visited countless investment properties. I vetted the economy. I looked at the rental numbers. I rolled up my sleeves to understand what's behind the numbers in this market and it paid off big time. To do that, it takes time, energy and resources and it takes the commitment. You need to do the same. You need to take this process of due diligence seriously and really know your market. Pick the right properties and get positive cash flow. You can get away with knowing a little less. If you have cash flow, at least you know you're in a right market that's going to be even better in the years to come. Remember to be a successful. As a real estate investor, you have to know one, that you want to be in this business and two, that you can hold on to your properties through thick and thin. This is like a marriage. Know that you want to be with this person when you get old.
